reviewer: new-demo build wave (in-browser runnability proof first, then headless-Chrome real download + real inference on the index page via CDP driver, mobile+desktop overflow + console/network checks across all 5 pages, bicubic-vs-Real-ESRGAN visual review)

rubric.evidence: REAL in-browser inference proven in a Web Worker BEFORE building: an ORT-web@1.21.0 WASM harness (headless Chrome, no GPU) created a session from real_esrgan_x4.onnx and ran it — input 'input' [1,3,116,160] f32 → output 'output' [1,3,464,640] f32 (exactly 4×). The GAN result is far sharper than a bicubic 4× of the same thumbnail (Laplacian-variance sharpness 6.2 → 384 on the index run) and cleans JPEG blocking, proving real detail synthesis not identity/noise. transformers.js has no Real-ESRGAN/RRDBNet class and no super-resolution pipeline task, so the demo runs the ONNX graph directly via raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module Worker. Index build-verify (CDP driver): fresh profile → Download (~67 MB) → ready → Upscale produced real output, 128×92 → 512×368, inference 9807 ms WASM, 0 console errors, 0 network failures.
rubric.notes: Real-ESRGAN x4plus (RRDBNet, ICCVW 2021) is a GAN trained on real-world blind degradation — distinct architecture from the built transformer-based Swin2SR (and 4× not 2×).

rubric.evidence: Output shape + semantic sanity confirmed: output dims [1,3,4h,4w] read back at runtime (464×640 for a 116×160 input). Semantic check: the Real-ESRGAN result is measurably sharper than a bicubic upscale at the SAME output resolution (Laplacian-variance rises sharply), and the 'See inside' detail-gain map (|Real-ESRGAN − bicubic|) lights up along edges/texture and stays dark on flat regions — proving the gain tracks structure, not uniform noise.
rubric.notes: image-to-image task template (dense per-pixel output grounded in the input) fits: a 4× larger image reconstructed from the small one.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance names the exact model (SceneWorks/real-esrgan-onnx, linked), task (image-to-image / super-resolution), runtime (onnxruntime-web 1.21), input [1,3,h,w] / output [1,3,4h,4w], dtype (fp32, plain WASM EP, no WebGPU), size (~67 MB) and licence (BSD-3-Clause), and honestly notes transformers.js has no Real-ESRGAN class. inference-off-main-thread = pass (new Worker + worker.js). No invented API.
rubric.notes: Input is bounded (long side ≤ maxIn) up front so a 4× pass stays feasible on single-threaded WASM; the copy explains this.

rubric.evidence: Every page loads through createModelLoader. The worker fetches the ONNX THROUGH Cache Storage (real-esrgan-onnx-cache) with a content-length Response so lib/model-cache.js scans '/SceneWorks/real-esrgan-onnx/' and the shared loader auto-inits on a returning visit (no Download button); the per-model clear-cache control works. First-visit driver run showed the honest Download (~67 MB) state, streaming progress, then ready.
rubric.notes: All 4 ladder pages share the same modelId → shared cache → auto-init; the multi-model page loads a SECOND model (DDColor) through its own loader.

rubric.evidence: loads-clean = pass (0 console errors on load across all 5 pages). Download progress streams from the worker into the loader progress bar; failures surface in the .status.err line and the loader offers Retry. Status uses role=status aria-live=polite. runs-without-webgpu-fallback: WASM-only (requiresWebGPU:false) — runs everywhere, verified on the no-GPU headless runner.
rubric.notes: The source-size and JPEG controls only rebuild the preview (cheap); inference runs only on Upscale.

rubric.evidence: Every control exercised and labelled + keyboard-operable: the rights-safe example gallery, the drop-zone + file input, the Upscale / Download buttons, the source-size and JPEG-compression <input type=range> sliders, and the before/after reveal (a native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext 'N% Real-ESRGAN'). Sliders live-update the preview; the demo shrinks a bounded thumbnail so the enlargement is meaningful (real small/compressed uploads are supported as-is).
rubric.notes: The comparison is honest by design — bicubic vs Real-ESRGAN at the SAME output size, not thumbnail vs big.

rubric.evidence: The upscaled image, the bicubic baseline, the detail-gain map, and the sharpness numbers all come from the on-device ORT-web run — no canned values. The output changes with the source size, compression and content (visible in the wipe and the map), proving it tracks the real model.
rubric.notes: The wild page states plainly that at tiny source sizes Real-ESRGAN INVENTS plausible detail rather than recovering it — an enhancement, not ground truth.

rubric.evidence: images-have-alt = pass, buttons-have-names = pass, live-status-region = pass. Canvases carry role=img + descriptive aria-label (bicubic upscale / Real-ESRGAN upscale / detail-gain); the reveal slider is a labelled native range with aria-valuetext; the size and JPEG sliders have <label>s; gallery thumbnails carry source/licence in their accessible name. Status is role=status aria-live=polite. :focus-visible styled on the reveal, drop-zone, and ranges.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied for the accessible comparison slider and canvas alternatives (see guidanceConsulted).

rubric.evidence: responsive-desktop = pass, responsive-mobile = pass. CDP measured horizontal overflow = 0 px at BOTH desktop 1280×800 and mobile 360×740 DPR3 on ALL 5 pages (index + basics/practical/wild/multi-model), 0 console errors, 0 network failures on load. Layout uses flex:1 1 300px + min-inline-size:0 two-column (image | controls) that stacks on mobile; the gallery is an auto-fill minmax grid; canvases are inline-size:100% block-size:auto; the reveal uses logical properties + touch-action:pan-y. Light + dark inherit design-system variables.
rubric.notes: responsive-check.mjs pre-scan: desktop clean · mobile clean.

rubric.evidence: Inference AND the dense output composite (clamp tensor → RGBA, bicubic baseline, detail-gain map — the systemic hotspot invariant 15 warns about, worse here because the output is 4× the input area) run in the module Worker; the three result images are built with OffscreenCanvas and returned as transferred ImageBitmaps (zero-copy). The main thread only draws bitmaps, so no long task during interaction.
rubric.notes: Guidance applied: break-up-long-tasks / identify-inp-causes — the per-pixel work is off the main thread and transferred, not cloned; input bounded so the 4× pass stays feasible.

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible before after image comparison slider keyboard labels aria-valuetext, canvas role img alt, live status region, range label
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Prefer native controls with explicit <label>; a range input is keyboard-operable and should announce its value (aria-valuetext); give informative canvases role=img + a descriptive name; use a single polite live region; style :focus-visible.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — the before/after divider is a native <input type=range> with aria-valuetext ('N% Real-ESRGAN'); the source-size and JPEG ranges have <label>s; every canvas has role=img + a descriptive aria-label; a single role=status aria-live=polite reports progress/results; :focus-visible rings throughout.

guidanceConsulted.query: responsive two column control panel no horizontal overflow flexbox intrinsic sizing logical properties
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use flexbox with flex:1 1 <basis> + min-inline-size:0 so wide flex items shrink instead of overflowing; size media with inline-size:100% block-size:auto; use logical properties; auto-fill/minmax grids for galleries.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — image/controls columns use flex:1 1 300px with min-inline-size:0 and stack on narrow viewports; the example gallery is a repeat(auto-fill,minmax()) grid; canvases and the reveal are inline-size:100% block-size:auto. Verified 0 px horizontal overflow at 360×740 and 1280×800 on all 5 pages.

guidanceConsulted.query: keep INP low move image inference and dense per-pixel post-processing off main thread web worker transfer ImageBitmap
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move long main-thread JavaScript (inference + dense pixel loops) into a worker; transfer buffers/ImageBitmaps instead of cloning.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: Applied — ONNX inference, the RGBA clamp, the bicubic baseline, and the detail-gain map (the dense-output hotspot, 4× the input area) all run in a module worker; results are transferred back as ImageBitmaps. No blocking main-thread pixel loop during interaction.

openQuestions: Real-ESRGAN accepts dynamic input; a tiled path with overlap would let large images be upscaled at full resolution rather than bounding the input to keep WASM memory feasible.
A WebGPU ORT-web EP path would cut the ~10 s WASM 4× pass substantially — worth a progressive-enhancement follow-up once verifiable on a GPU runner.

followUpGoals.goal: Add a tiled super-resolution path (overlapping tiles) so full-resolution images can be enlarged without bounding the input.
Add a multi-model 'restore then enlarge' ladder demo chaining NAFNet deblur → Real-ESRGAN upscale.

summary: New demo: on-device 4× SUPER-RESOLUTION via Real-ESRGAN x4plus (SceneWorks/real-esrgan-onnx, BSD-3-Clause) run through raw ONNX Runtime Web in a module worker, with the dense output composite (clamp + bicubic baseline + detail-gain map) off the main thread and transferred as ImageBitmaps. Runnability was proven first in a headless-Chrome Web Worker (input [1,3,h,w] → output [1,3,4h,4w], sharpness far above a bicubic upscale of the same thumbnail); the index + 4 ladder pages (basics shrink-then-upscale / practical rescue-compression+download / wild find-the-invention-limit / multi-model enlarge→DDColor colourise) were build-verified with real download + inference, an honest bicubic-vs-Real-ESRGAN reveal, cached auto-init, clean console, and 0 px overflow on mobile+desktop across all 5 pages. Sample images come from the rights-safe media library; the source is shrunk at a bounded scale so the enlargement is meaningful, and real small/compressed images can be uploaded. Distinct from the built Swin2SR: a GAN (RRDBNet) not a transformer, 4× not 2×, real-world blind degradation.
